surviving.title: Transaction.Reset() preserves transaction-level options (read_system_keys, RYW-disable, timeout, etc.) that libfdb_c's reset() clears back to database defaults
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1785 (Reset entry); the actual option-preservation site is reset() at transaction.go:2758-2810, see the "Preserved across reset" comment at :2806-2809; the misleading doc claim is at :1779
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2735 ReadYourWritesTransaction::reset() — persistentOptions.clear() :2744, sensitivePersistentOptions.clear() :2745, options.reset(tr) :2746 (memset in ReadYourWritesTransactionOptions::reset :2077-2082), re-copy getTransactionDefaults() :2749-2751, resetRyow() :2752; non-persistent options also cleared on the onError path via RYWImpl::onError :1521 -> resetRyow() :2699 -> options.reset(tr) :2721; persistent markers in vexillographer/fdb.options lines 294/298/302/351 (only timeout/retry_limit/max_retry_delay/auth_token)
surviving.description: Go's user-facing Reset() (transaction.go:1785) calls the internal reset() (transaction.go:2728), which does NOT touch readSystemKeys/writeSystemKeys (set at :1172-1181), rywDisabled, bypassUnreadable, lockAware, readLockAware, sizeLimit, priority, causalReadRisky, snapshotRYWDisableCount, tags, timeout, retryLimit, maxRetryDelay -- all are preserved (see the 'Preserved across reset' comment at :2776). libfdb_c's ReadYourWritesTransaction::reset() instead CLEARS persistentOptions+sensitivePersistentOptions, runs options.reset(tr) (memset of ReadYourWritesTransactionOptions -> readSystemKeys=writeSystemKeys=readYourWritesDisabled=false, sizeLimit/priority back to default), then re-populates persistentOptions ONLY from the Database-level transaction defaults. So after a user reset(): (a) non-persistent transaction-level options (read_system_keys, access_system_keys, read_your_writes_disable, lock_aware, bypass_unreadable, size_limit, priority, causal_read_risky, tags) revert to DB defaults; and (b) even the 4 persistent options (timeout/retry_limit/max_retry_delay/auth_token) revert to DB defaults because the per-transaction setOption entries were cleared. Go keeps them all. The Reset() doc comment claims it matches C++ 'ReadYourWritesTransaction::reset() + applyPersistentOptions' -- it is the opposite.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ reset() (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2735-2755) is explicit: persistentOptions.clear(); sensitivePersistentOptions.clear(); options.reset(tr) (memset, :2078-2083); then std::copy(getTransactionDefaults()..., back_inserter(persistentOptions)); resetRyow() re-applies only those DB defaults. read_system_keys (code 302) / access_system_keys (301) / read_your_writes_disable (51) / size_limit (503) are NOT marked persistent in fdb.options, so they are gone after reset. Go preserves them.
surviving.repro:
Differential, single transaction handle reused via Reset():
1) tx := db.CreateTransaction(); tx.SetReadVersion(rv)  // pin RV so GRV variance is out of scope
2) tx.SetReadSystemKeys()                                 // read_system_keys = true
3) tx.Reset()                                             // both clients: reset the handle
4) tx.SetReadVersion(rv)                                  // Reset cleared the user RV; re-pin for a clean eager check
5) _, err := tx.Get(ctx, []byte{0xff,'f','o','o'})        // key = \xff"foo"
Expected: libfdb_c err == FDBError(2004) key_outside_legal_range (read_system_keys cleared by reset, getMaxReadKey()=\xff). Go err == nil with value nil (readSystemKeys preserved, maxReadKey()=\xff\xff so \xff"foo" passes the legal-range gate). Secondary shapes from the same root cause: (a) tx.SetReadYourWritesDisable(); tx.Reset(); tx.Set(k,v); tx.Get(k) — libfdb_c re-enables RYW so the read sees v, Go keeps rywDisabled so it does not; (b) tx.Options().SetSizeLimit(100); tx.Reset(); commit a >100-byte txn — libfdb_c accepts (limit reverted to 10MB default), Go rejects with transaction_too_large.
surviving.verifierReasoning:
Independently verified on both sides.

GO: Transaction.Reset() (transaction.go:1785-1798) clears only retryCount/backoff/creationTime/metricStart then calls reset(). reset() (transaction.go:2758-2810) does NOT clear readSystemKeys/writeSystemKeys (fields at :399-400, set at :1174/:1179-1180), rywDisabled, sizeLimit, priority, lockAware, readLockAware, causalReadRisky, maxRetryDelay, retryLimit, timeout — the explicit comment at :2806-2809 ("Preserved across reset") confirms this. maxReadKey() (:1128-1133) returns \xff\xff when readSystemKeys is set, else \xff; Get() (:704-709) returns FDBError 2004 when key >= maxReadKey().

C++: ReadYourWritesTransaction::reset() (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2735) runs persistentOptions.clear() (:2744), sensitivePersistentOptions.clear() (:2745), options.reset(tr) (:2746 -> ReadYourWritesTransactionOptions::reset :2077-2082 = memset(this,0,sizeof) so readSystemKeys=writeSystemKeys=readYourWritesDisabled=false, sizeLimit/priority to default), then re-copies ONLY getTransactionDefaults() into persistentOptions (:2749-2751) and resetRyow() (:2752) re-applies only those DB defaults. Only timeout/retry_limit/max_retry_delay/auth_token are persistent="true" in fdb.options (:294/298/302/351); read_system_keys/access_system_keys/read_your_writes_disable/size_limit/priority/lock_aware are NOT persistent, so they are gone after reset(). Stronger still: RYWImpl::onError (:1499) also calls resetRyow() (:1521) -> options.reset(tr) (:2721), so C++ clears the non-persistent options on BOTH the onError-retry and user-reset paths; Go's single shared reset() preserves them on both.

Concrete divergence on input "SetReadSystemKeys(); Reset(); Get({0xff,'f','o','o'})": Go keeps readSystemKeys=true -> maxReadKey()=\xff\xff -> \xff"foo" < \xff\xff -> eager 2004 check passes -> Get returns nil,nil (key absent). libfdb_c: reset() cleared read_system_keys -> getMaxReadKey()=\xff -> \xff"foo" >= \xff -> getValue throws key_outside_legal_range (2004). This is an over-permission divergence (Go can read/write \xff system keys a libfdb_c handle would reject after reset); the same mechanism makes a previously-set restrictive size_limit persist (Go rejects a commit C++ accepts) and a BATCH priority persist across reset. No existing test pins it: the system-key tests (c_binding_port_test.go:2660+) never reset-then-recheck, and TestSetTimeout_ResetRestartsTimer (:1508) re-applies SetTimeout after Reset so it cannot observe preservation. The finder's cited lines are essentially exact (options.reset at :2746 vs cited :2747; memset at :2077-2078 as cited). Severity high: correctness- and safety-relevant on a shared cluster (silent system-key over-permission + divergent commit acceptance), though it requires the reuse-after-Reset pattern so medium is also defensible.

surviving.title: Hedged read leaks the primary's QueueModel startRequest delta when ctx is cancelled during the initial primary-wait
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/hedge.go:99-102 (the ctx.Done() branch of sendFrameWithHedge's 3-way select; the leak is the return at line 102)
surviving.cppReference: n/a — Go-intrinsic accounting leak. The conceptual spec is C++ LoadBalance.actor.h, where ModelHolder (RAII) releases the QueueModel delta for EVERY started request — winner, cancelled laggard, or actor-cancelled alike — so an early abort can never leave a startRequest unbalanced. The codebase's own RFC-010 #5 invariant (pinned at readpath.go:317-319) mirrors this.
surviving.description: In sendFrameWithHedge, once a secondary exists the function blocks in a 3-way select waiting for {primary reply, hedge timer, ctx.Done}. On the ctx.Done branch it does pResult.replyHandle.Cancel()/Release() and returns `hedgeResult{err: ctx.Err()}` — with addr="", delta=0, start=zero, others=nil. But the primary request was already counted: makeSender called tx.db.queueModel.startRequest(addr) and stored the returned delta in pResult.delta. The caller (sendGetValue/sendGetRange/sendGetKey) only calls endRequest for result.others (empty) and for result.addr when non-empty (it is ""). So the primary's startRequest delta is never matched by an endRequest. smoothOutstanding.total for that server is incremented and never decremented; the smoother's estimate converges to the inflated total and stays elevated by >=delta (penalty, >=1.0) forever. Every sibling branch returns the account correctly: waitForReply's ctx.Done (hedge.go:137) sets addr/delta/start, and raceReplies' ctx.Done (hedge.go:174) puts both arms in others. Only sendFrameWithHedge's own ctx.Done branch drops it. Fix: return hedgeResult{addr: pResult.addr, delta: pResult.delta, start: pResult.start, err: ctx.Err()} so the caller ends it exactly once (identical to waitForReply's ctx.Done branch).
surviving.whyWrong: The codebase pins this exact invariant: 'End every started request ... exactly once, else its QueueModel delta leaks permanently and biases server selection. RFC-010 #5' (readpath.go:511-513). waitForReply and raceReplies both carry the account on ctx.Done; sendFrameWithHedge's own ctx.Done branch is the one omission. The leak is permanent (QueueModel lives on the long-lived Database) and compounds: each cancellation in the pre-hedge window adds another +penalty to that server's smoothTotal, progressively de-prioritizing it in chooseServer/chooseTopTwo until it is effectively always the last pick.
surviving.repro: Unit-level differential (no container needed): qm := newQueueModel(); base := totalOf(qm,"primary"); construct a primary sendFunc that calls d := qm.startRequest("primary") and returns inFlightRPC{replyCh: make(chan transport.Response) /* never replies */, replyHandle: &transport.ReplyHandle{}, addr:"primary", delta:d, start: time.Now()}; provide a NON-nil secondary sendFunc (e.g. returns inFlightRPC with a never-ready replyCh) so secondary != nil; ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(...); cancel(); res := sendFrameWithHedge(ctx, 1*time.Second /* hedge timer must not fire */, primary, secondary, 5*time.Second); applyCallerAccounting(qm, res). Assert totalOf(qm,"primary") ~ base (1e-9). Today it FAILS: total stays elevated by the startRequest penalty (~1.0) because res.addr=="" and res.others==nil, so applyCallerAccounting issues no endRequest. With the one-line fix it returns to baseline. This is exactly the totalOf sentinel pattern already used by TestHedge_QueueModelOutstandingReturnsToBaseline, just driving sendFrameWithHedge's secondary!=nil + pre-hedge ctx.Done path that no current test reaches.
surviving.verifierReasoning: I read the full sendFrameWithHedge (hedge.go:51-104) and its siblings. At hedge.go:59 the primary is sent via primary() = makeSender(...), which at readpath.go:286 calls tx.db.queueModel.startRequest(server.Address), capturing the returned delta into pResult.delta (readpath.go:298-304). startRequest (loadbalance.go:274-282) does d.smoothOutstanding.addDelta(+delta) — adding the server's penalty (>=1.0) to smoother.total (addDelta at loadbalance.go:349-352). When secondary != nil (multi-replica + hedge enabled, gated at readpath.go:244-247 via chooseTopTwo + hedgeEnabled.Load()), control reaches the 3-way select. On the ctx.Done() arm (hedge.go:99-102) it Cancels/Releases the reply handle and returns hedgeResult{err: ctx.Err()} with addr="", delta=0, start=zero, others=nil. The caller (readpath.go:320-333, identical at 514-527 and 886-899) then: iterates result.others (empty -> no endRequest); checks result.addr != "" (false -> skips endRequest); sees result.err != nil and returns at readpath.go:332-333 before endRequestFull (337). So the primary's +delta is never subtracted. It is removed only via addDelta(-delta) at loadbalance.go:304 inside endRequest/endRequestFull, which never runs. smoother.total stays permanently elevated by >=1.0, and smoothTotal (loadbalance.go:342-344) converges estimate toward that inflated total forever; QueueModel lives on the long-lived Database, so the bias is permanent and compounds per cancellation, lowering that server's rank in chooseServer (loadbalance.go:151-152) and chooseTopTwo (231). Every sibling exit carries the account correctly: waitForReply's ctx.Done sets addr/delta/start (hedge.go:134-137) and raceReplies' ctx.Done puts both arms in others (hedge.go:169-175). Only sendFrameWithHedge's own ctx.Done branch (line 102) drops it. The fix is to return hedgeResult{addr: pResult.addr, delta: pResult.delta, start: pResult.start, err: ctx.Err()}, identical to waitForReply's ctx.Done branch, so the caller's result.addr!=\"\" && result.err!=nil path (readpath.go:328-329) ends it exactly once. I verified no existing test catches it: TestHedge_ContextCancellation (hedge_test.go:125-145) passes secondary=nil, which short-circuits to waitForReply at hedge.go:72-74 before the 3-way select, and asserts only result.err — it never reaches the buggy branch nor checks any QueueModel total. The Baseline test's 'context cancel' subtest (hedge_test.go:269-284) and TestWaitForReply_AccountsSingleRequest (314-341) call raceReplies and waitForReply directly, not sendFrameWithHedge, so the pre-hedge ctx.Done arm of sendFrameWithHedge is entirely unexercised.

surviving.title: Hedge ctx.Done() branch leaks the in-flight primary's QueueModel startRequest delta
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/hedge.go:99-102 (the top-level select's `case <-ctx.Done()` in sendFrameWithHedge); leak observed via the caller's accounting at pkg/fdbgo/client/readpath.go:316-338 (and identically at 509-562, 881-903)
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbrpc/include/fdbrpc/LoadBalance.actor.h:67 (~ModelHolder destructor releases on cancel) calling release() at lines 59-63 (release -> QueueModel::endRequest), paired with the addRequest in the ctor at line 55 — RAII guarantees every started request is ended on the cancellation path, which the Go top-level ctx.Done branch fails to replicate
surviving.description: In sendFrameWithHedge, after the primary send succeeds (pResult.err==nil, so QueueModel.startRequest was called and pResult.delta is live) and a secondary exists, the top-level select waits for the primary reply or the hedge timer. Its `case <-ctx.Done()` (hedge.go:99) returns `hedgeResult{err: ctx.Err()}` with addr="", delta=0, start=zero, others=nil. The caller (sendGetValue/sendGetKey/sendGetRange) only ends requests found in result.others (empty) or guarded by `if result.addr != ""` (false), so the primary's started delta is NEVER passed to endRequest. startRequest adds `delta` to that server's smoothOutstanding (loadbalance.go:279) and only endRequest removes it (loadbalance.go:304), so the delta stays in smoothOutstanding permanently. The sibling waitForReply (hedge.go:137) and raceReplies (hedge.go:172-174) ctx.Done branches correctly propagate addr/delta/start; only this top-level branch drops it.
surviving.whyWrong: The hedge accounting contract (hedge.go:29-37) requires every started request to be recoverable for endRequest via EITHER addr/delta/start (the winner/single) OR others; here the started primary is in neither. Existing TestHedge_ContextCancellation (hedge_test.go:125) only exercises secondary==nil, which routes through waitForReply (correctly accounted), so this multi-server (hedge-enabled) ctx.Done sub-shape is untested. Fix: return hedgeResult{addr: pResult.addr, delta: pResult.delta, start: pResult.start, err: ctx.Err()} exactly as waitForReply does at hedge.go:137.
surviving.repro: Direct unit test on sendFrameWithHedge that deterministically hits the top-level ctx.Done branch: qm := newQueueModel(); base := qm.getOrCreate("primary").smoothOutstanding.total; ctx,cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background()); cancel() // pre-cancel; primary := func() inFlightRPC { return inFlightRPC{ replyCh: make(chan transport.Response) /*never replies*/, replyHandle: &transport.ReplyHandle{}, addr: "primary", delta: qm.startRequest("primary"), start: time.Now() } }; secondary := func() inFlightRPC { return mkInflight("secondary", 1.0, false) }; res := sendFrameWithHedge(ctx, 1*time.Second /*long so timer.C never fires first*/, primary, secondary, 5*time.Second); applyCallerAccounting(qm, res). At entry only ctx.Done is ready (primary never replies, 1s timer not fired), so the select deterministically takes hedge.go:99 and returns hedgeResult{err: context.Canceled} with addr=="". EXPECT FAILS TODAY: res.addr should be "primary" (is "") and qm.getOrCreate("primary").smoothOutstanding.total should return to base within 1e-9 (instead stays inflated by the leaked primary delta). After the fix (return {addr:pResult.addr, delta:pResult.delta, start:pResult.start, err:ctx.Err()} like waitForReply hedge.go:137), res.addr=="primary" and total returns to baseline.
surviving.verifierReasoning: Read Go hedge.go:51-104 and readpath.go:257-338. In sendFrameWithHedge, `pResult := primary()` (hedge.go:59) runs makeSender's closure, which calls `tx.db.queueModel.startRequest(server.Address)` (readpath.go:286) — adding `delta` to that server's smoothOutstanding.total (loadbalance.go:274-282) — and on SendFrame success returns inFlightRPC{addr,delta,start, err=nil}. With a non-nil secondary (hedge default ON, db.hedgeEnabled.Store(true) at database.go:786; multi-replica shard so chooseTopTwo returns secondIdx>=0, readpath.go:244-247), control reaches the top-level select (hedge.go:82). The `case <-ctx.Done()` (hedge.go:99-102) returns `hedgeResult{err: ctx.Err()}` with addr=="" / delta==0 / others==nil. The caller ends requests ONLY from result.others (readpath.go:320, empty) and inside `if result.addr != ""` (readpath.go:324, false). So the primary's startRequest delta is never passed to endRequest. endRequest is the only path that does addDelta(-delta) (loadbalance.go:304); the smoother's `total` therefore stays permanently inflated by delta (>=1.0 penalty) and smoothTotal converges estimate->total, biasing chooseServer/chooseTopTwo away from that healthy server. Repeated cancelled hedged reads accumulate without bound. The sibling branches prove the inconsistency: waitForReply's ctx.Done returns {addr,delta,start,err} (hedge.go:134-137) and raceReplies' ctx.Done returns others:[a,b] (hedge.go:169-174); only this top-level branch drops accounting. C++ confirms the contract: ModelHolder is RAII — ctor calls addRequest (LoadBalance.actor.h:55), and ~ModelHolder (line 67) calls release->endRequest (lines 59-63) on EVERY path including actor cancellation, so libfdb_c never leaks the outstanding count on cancel; the Go port must do it explicitly and this branch omits it. Untested: TestHedge_ContextCancellation (hedge_test.go:125-145) uses secondary=nil, routing through waitForReply (correctly accounted); the raceReplies/waitForReply accounting tests (hedge_test.go:184-285) call leaf functions directly and never drive sendFrameWithHedge's top-level select with a non-nil secondary + cancelled ctx, so hedge.go:99 has no accounting coverage. The finder's Go location (hedge.go:99-102) and mechanism are exactly correct; only the cppReference needed correcting from "n/a" to the concrete ModelHolder RAII proof.

surviving.title: Watch() skips the eager legal-range + key-size validation libfdb_c performs (watch on a \xff system key or oversized key succeeds in Go, 2004/2102 in C++)
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/readpath.go:1069 (func WatchSetup) — the missing checks belong right after the rywDisabled gate at line 1074, mirroring C++'s rywDisabled → legal-range → key-too-large ordering
surviving.cppReference: fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2451-2456 (legal-range at 2451-2452, key_too_large at 2454-2456); helpers getMaxReadKey at 2757-2762, RYWImpl::watch actor at 1284 confirms no re-check
surviving.description: C++ ReadYourWritesTransaction::watch rejects, BEFORE registering the watch, (a) any key >= allKeys.end (\xff\xff) or (key >= getMaxReadKey() && key != metadataVersionKey) with key_outside_legal_range (2004), and (b) any key larger than getMaxWriteKeySize(key, rawAccess) with key_too_large (2102). Go's WatchSetup performs NEITHER check: it only gates on rywDisabled (watches_disabled 1034) and ensureReadVersion, then calls AddReadConflictKey(key) and tx.ryw.get(ctx,key,tx.getValue). ryw.get has no maxReadKey/legal-range gate (the only legal-range check lives in Transaction.Get, which WatchSetup bypasses), and the fdb facade Watch adds none either. So a watch on an illegal key is set up instead of being rejected, and the system-key read succeeds because the legal-range check is purely client-side (the storage server serves \xff keys).
surviving.whyWrong: C++ proves the eager rejection at ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2451-2452 (`if (key >= allKeys.end || (key >= getMaxReadKey() && key != metadataVersionKey && tr.apiVersionAtLeast(300))) return key_outside_legal_range();`) and :2454-2456 (`if (key.size() > getMaxWriteKeySize(...)) return key_too_large();`). allKeys.end == systemKeys.end == \xff\xff (SystemData.cpp:34), getMaxReadKey() == normalKeys.end (\xff) without READ/ACCESS_SYSTEM_KEYS (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2757-2762). Go readpath.go WatchSetup (lines 1069-1112) has no analogous guard.
surviving.repro: Differential test, both clients, in a transaction WITHOUT ACCESS_SYSTEM_KEYS/READ_SYSTEM_KEYS at the harness default API version (>=300). Case A (2004): tx.Watch([]byte("\xff\x05")); bounded-wait on the future (ctx deadline ~200ms). libfdb_c: w.Get() returns FDBError code 2004 (key_outside_legal_range) immediately; Go: WatchSetup returns nil and the future stays pending (resolves to nil/fires or ctx-cancelled, never 2004) — assert goCode==cgoCode fails. Case B (2102): tx.Watch on a NON-system key of length > 10000 bytes (e.g. bytes.Repeat([]byte("a"),10001)); libfdb_c: w.Get() returns 2102 (key_too_large) immediately; Go: pending, no 2102. Pattern mirrors the existing TestDifferential_WatchRYWDisabled (compare fdbErrorCode(w.Get()) between goClient and cgoClient), but with a short ctx so the Go pending-future case is observable rather than blocking.
surviving.verifierReasoning: I read the full Go WatchSetup and the full C++ watch(). GO: Transaction.Watch (readpath.go:1044) calls WatchSetup (readpath.go:1069), which gates ONLY on tx.rywDisabled→1034 (lines 1072-1074), then ensureReadVersion, AddReadConflictKey(key) (1090), and value,err := tx.ryw.get(ctx,key,tx.getValue) (1101). I verified ryw.get (ryw.go:370) has only the RFC-098 unreadable gate — no legal-range/size check — and tx.getValue/getValueImpl (readpath.go:381/401) only locate+send the GetValue RPC. The legal-range check (bytes.Compare(key, tx.maxReadKey())>=0 && !metadataVersionKey → FDBError{2004}) exists ONLY in Transaction.Get (transaction.go:708-709), which the Watch path never calls. maxReadKey() (transaction.go:1128-1133) returns \xff without system-key access. There is NO key-size (2102) check anywhere in the watch path. CPP: ReadYourWritesTransaction::watch (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2440) performs, BEFORE RYWImpl::watch: line 2451-2452 `if (key >= allKeys.end || (key >= getMaxReadKey() && key != metadataVersionKey && tr.apiVersionAtLeast(300))) return key_outside_legal_range();` and 2454-2456 `if (key.size() > getMaxWriteKeySize(key, ...options.rawAccess)) return key_too_large();`. getMaxReadKey() (2757-2762) returns normalKeys.end (\xff) without readSystemKeys. The RYWImpl::watch actor (1284-1298) does NOT repeat these checks, so the public watch() eager check is the sole gate. Concrete divergence on input key=\xff\x05 with no ACCESS/READ_SYSTEM_KEYS at apiVersion>=300: C++ returns key_outside_legal_range (2004) from the watch future immediately (\xff\x05 >= getMaxReadKey()=\xff, != metadataVersionKey); Go's WatchSetup returns err==nil and registers a pending watch (storage serves \xff keys; the gate is purely client-side). Likewise key.size()>10000 on a non-system key: C++ 2102, Go proceeds. I grepped pkg/fdbgo/bench/differential_watch_test.go and client/*_test.go: the only watch-error test is WatchRYWDisabled (1034); no test pins 2004/2102 on a watch, so this is unpinned. Not on the known-resolved list (that list covers Get/getRange/getKey/raw-access key limits — the SVK/getKey legal-range work — but NOT the watch entry point).

surviving.title: Transaction-level snapshot-RYW-disable survives reset()/retry in Go; libfdb_c discards it and re-seeds the DB default (snapshot reads see pending writes again after a retry)
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:2758-2810 (reset() preserves snapshotRYWDisableCount by omission; preserved-options comment at 2806-2809) and the false "persistent option" field comment at transaction.go:384-393; bypass at transaction.go:487/529/551/511; SetSnapshotRYWDisable at 2557-2558; OnError at 1988→reset(); facade manual-loop path pkg/fdbgo/fdb/transaction.go:348-354 (OnError delegates to inner.OnError with no re-seed) with the design comment at 381-383; raw auto-retry loop pkg/fdbgo/client/database.go:~813-833 (no re-seed). Test enshrining the wrong behavior: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction_unit_test.go:525-526.
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/vexillographer/fdb.options:312-315 (transaction-level snapshot_ryw_enable/disable codes 600/601, NO persistent="true"); fdb.options:294/298/302 (only timeout/retry_limit/max_retry_delay are persistent). /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2521-2530 (setOption gates persistentOptions on opt.persistent), :2596 (SNAPSHOT_RYW_DISABLE→snapshotRywEnabled--), :1521 (onError→resetRyow), :2718-2723 (resetRyow→options.reset(tr)+applyPersistentOptions for API>=16), :2078-2082 (options.reset re-seeds snapshotRywEnabled=db default), :2678-2697 (applyPersistentOptions replays only persistent options), :402 (snapshot bypass iff snapshotRywEnabled<=0). /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/NativeAPI.actor.cpp:1603 (db default snapshotRywEnabled=hasSnapshotRYW()?1:0). /tmp/fdbsrc/flow/ApiVersions.cmake:6 (FDB_AV_SNAPSHOT_RYW=300).
surviving.description: Go's reset() keeps snapshotRYWDisableCount unchanged on retry, so a transaction-level SetSnapshotRYWDisable() (FDB_TR_OPTION_SNAPSHOT_RYW_DISABLE) stays in effect across all retry attempts. libfdb_c treats codes 26/27 as NON-persistent options: on every reset (incl. the onError retry path) it re-seeds the per-txn snapshotRywEnabled from the database default (1=enabled for API>=300) and does NOT replay the per-txn disable. Result after a retry: Go snapshot reads still bypass the RYW write map (don't see the txn's own pending writes), while libfdb_c snapshot reads are RYW-enabled and DO see them. The Go in-code comment that asserts this is 'a persistent option ... like C++ persistentOptions' is factually wrong against the C++ spec.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ proof: snapshot_ryw_enable/disable lack persistent="true" in fdb.options:183-186, so applyPersistentOptions (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2678-2697) never replays them; resetRyow (:2721-2724) re-seeds options.snapshotRywEnabled = db->snapshotRywEnabled (:2082 / NativeAPI.actor.cpp:1603 = 1 enabled). Go instead preserves the disable counter across reset() (transaction.go:2776-2779), so the option is sticky where C++ drops it.
surviving.repro: Raw client (API>=510), cleanest: tx := db.CreateTransaction(); tx.SetSnapshotRYWDisable() (count=1). tx.Set("k","v"); tx.Snapshot().Get(ctx,"k") -> nil (bypass; matches C++ at this point). Force a retryable error: tx.OnError(ctx, &wire.FDBError{Code:1020}) -> reset(); count PRESERVED=1. tx.Set("k","v"); got,_ := tx.Snapshot().Get(ctx,"k"). Go: got==nil (count still 1 -> bypass -> does NOT see the txn's own pending write). libfdb_c: got=="v" (resetRyow re-seeds snapshotRywEnabled to db default 1 -> RYW active -> sees the pending write). Differential harness (gofdb vs cgofdb): drive a manual CreateTransaction + OnError loop with a transaction-level SetSnapshotRywDisable() set once before the loop; force exactly one retryable error (e.g. not_committed 1020 via a conflicting sibling txn, or fault injection); after the retry, snapshot-read the txn's own pending write. Go returns absent; libfdb_c returns the pending value. Regression: also flip client/transaction_unit_test.go:525-526, which currently asserts the divergent behavior (snapshotRYWDisableCount must be re-seeded to the DB net, not preserved, across the internal onError reset).
surviving.verifierReasoning:
Confirmed by reading both sides in full. GO: client/transaction.go:2758-2810 reset() never touches snapshotRYWDisableCount and the preserved-options comment at 2806-2809 explicitly lists it; the field comment at 384-393 falsely calls it "a persistent option ... like C++ persistentOptions." SetSnapshotRYWDisable (2557-2558) does count++; the snapshot bypass fires iff snapshotRYWDisableCount>0 (487/529/551/511). OnError (1988) calls reset() (2051/2068/2096/2117). The facade fdb/transaction.go:348-354 OnError merely delegates to inner.OnError with no re-seed, and its own comment (381-383) admits the onError-retry reset "PRESERVES user options so retries keep them." applyTxDefaults (fdb/database.go:410) re-seeds via SetSnapshotRYWDisableCount(d.snapshotRywDisableNet) only inside the auto-retry closure and at CreateTransaction — NOT on a manual OnError retry, and the raw client.Database.Transact loop (~813-833) has no re-seed at all. So a transaction-level SetSnapshotRYWDisable() stays in effect across retries.

C++ proves the opposite. fdb.options:312-315 — the transaction-level snapshot_ryw_enable/disable (codes 600/601) lack persistent="true" (only timeout/retry_limit/max_retry_delay at :294/:298/:302 are persistent; the doc text on those even says all OTHER options "must be reset after a call to onError"). ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2521-2530 setOption adds to persistentOptions only when opt.persistent; :2596 SNAPSHOT_RYW_DISABLE just does snapshotRywEnabled--. onError (:1521) calls resetRyow, which for API>=16 (:2718-2723) runs options.reset(tr)+applyPersistentOptions(): options.reset (:2078-2082) re-seeds snapshotRywEnabled = tr.getDatabase()->snapshotRywEnabled, and applyPersistentOptions (:2678-2697) replays ONLY persistent options — so the non-persistent disable is dropped. The DB default is 1 (enabled) for API>=300 (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:1603 = hasSnapshotRYW()?1:0; ApiVersions.cmake:6 FDB_AV_SNAPSHOT_RYW=300), and the bypass fires iff snapshotRywEnabled<=0 (:402). Net: after one retry, Go count stays 1 (snapshot read bypasses RYW, does NOT see the txn's pending write) while libfdb_c snapshotRywEnabled is re-seeded to 1 (RYW active, snapshot read DOES see the pending write).

Coverage: the differential bench/differential_snapshot_ryw_test.go only toggles options within a single attempt (no OnError/reset). The unit test client/transaction_unit_test.go:525-526 actively pins the WRONG behavior ("snapshotRYWDisableCount must be preserved across reset — persistent option"). The retry/reset axis is unpinned for correctness, so this is a real, untested divergence. Finding inaccuracies are minor: the cited Go lines 2776-2779 are the mutation/conflict clears, not the preservation (which is by omission, documented at 2806-2809); the cited C++ fdb.options:183-186 are the DATABASE-level codes 26/27, whereas a transaction-level SetSnapshotRYWDisable maps to codes 600/601 at fdb.options:312-315 (both equally lack persistent); and the facade Transact-closure path does NOT drift (applyTxDefaults re-seeds first each attempt) — only the raw client.Database.Transact drifts. None of these change the verdict.

surviving.axisLabel: buffer pools, slice aliasing, integer overflow
surviving.title: Commit/read body buffer returned to sync.Pool while writeLoop may still reference it (SendFrame ctx.Done early-return)
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/commitpath.go:57 (marshalBufPool.Put on the SendFrame-error branch) is the cited and correct primary site. Mechanism: transport/conn.go:454-455 (SendFrame returns errConnClosed via second-select ctx.Done after the frame was enqueued at conn.go:439), combined with writeLoop still able to consume that frame at conn.go:518/536 and read body at transport/framing.go:71. Same defect at readpath.go:484 (getValueBufPool) and readpath.go:856 (getKeyValuesBufPool). Fix should mirror the existing errCh / SendFrameDeferred discipline: do NOT return the body buffer to the pool when SendFrame fails after the frame was enqueued (leave it to GC), or have SendFrame distinguish "never enqueued" from "enqueued but unconsumed".
surviving.cppReference: n/a — Go-intrinsic data race (sync.Pool reuse vs writeLoop reference), not a libfdb_c behavioral divergence.
surviving.description: buildCommitTransactionRequest hands the caller a pooled marshal buffer (marshalBufPool) as `body`, with `body` aliasing the pooled backing array. commit() returns it to the pool with marshalBufPool.Put(poolBuf) on the SendFrame error branch (commitpath.go:57) and the success branch (:68). The safety argument is "body is copied into WriteFrame's own buffer — safe to return to pool", which holds only when SendFrame has actually waited for writeLoop to consume the frame. But transport.Conn.SendFrame (conn.go:431) enqueues the frame onto the buffered writeCh (cap 256) and then, in its second select, returns errConnClosed via `<-c.ctx.Done()` (conn.go:454) WITHOUT waiting for the writeLoop ack. On that path the frame is still sitting in writeCh, and writeLoop (conn.go:514-546) — whose select now sees both the buffered frame and ctx.Done — can still pick the writeCh case and call WriteFrame(c.wbuf, req.token, req.body), reading `body`, after commit() has already Put poolBuf back to the global pool. A concurrent goroutine that Gets the same pooled buffer and runs MarshalFDBPooled into it then races writeLoop's read of the same backing array. Note the transport author already guards the ANALOGOUS hazard for errCh (deliberately NOT pooling errCh on the ctx.Done path, conn.go:447-449 "writeLoop may still hold a reference") and for the deferred read path (GetPipelined leaves poolBuf un-pooled, readpath.go:822 'can't pool body for SendFrameDeferred — writeLoop holds reference'), but the synchronous SendFrame ctx.Done early-return re-introduces exactly that reference-still-held window for the body buffer. The same pattern exists in sendGetRange (readpath.go:856).
surviving.whyWrong: Go memory model: a buffer Put back to a sync.Pool and then re-acquired+written by goroutine B is read concurrently by writeLoop on the torn-down connection — a data race (UB, -race detectable). The functional blast radius is limited (the garbled frame goes only to the connection whose ctx was just cancelled and is being discarded; goroutine B's own marshal output stays correct), but it is still a genuine pool-Put-while-referenced race in the commit hot path.
surviving.repro: A -race regression (intrinsic, no container needed if a loopback fake server is used): (1) Dial a Conn to a fake server. (2) Goroutine A: body,poolBuf := buildCommitTransactionRequest(...); enqueue via conn.SendFrame so the frame lands in writeCh; arrange the connection ctx to cancel the instant after enqueue (server drops the socket, or directly call the teardown) so SendFrame's second select (conn.go:454) returns errConnClosed; A then runs the error branch marshalBufPool.Put(poolBuf) (commitpath.go:57). (3) writeLoop's select (conn.go:518/536) picks the still-buffered frame and calls WriteFrame → framing.go:71 copy(..., body), reading body. (4) Concurrently goroutine B: buildCommitTransactionRequest again → marshalBufPool.Get() returns A's buffer → MarshalFDBPooled writes the same backing array in place. Run under `go test -race`: it reports a read (writeLoop WriteFrame) / write (B MarshalFDBPooled) data race on the shared backing array. A deterministic variant pins writeLoop mid-WriteFrame (busy on a prior frame) while A enqueues+cancels+Puts and B reuses, removing reliance on select randomness.
surviving.verifierReasoning:
Read both sides in full and the happens-before chain holds.

(1) body aliases the pooled buffer: commitpath.go:442-457 — `bufp := marshalBufPool.Get(); result := req.MarshalFDBPooled(*bufp); *bufp = result; return result, bufp`, so `body`(=result) and `*poolBuf`(=bufp) are the SAME backing array. Same construction for getValue (readpath.go:478) and getRange (readpath.go:817).

(2) SendFrame can return after the frame is already enqueued: transport/conn.go:438-443 enqueues onto writeCh (first select case taken), then conn.go:450-456 second select returns errConnClosed via `<-c.ctx.Done()` (line 454-455) WITHOUT receiving errCh. The author already declines to pool errCh on this very path (conn.go:447-449 comment) and declines to pool the deferred body for the analogous reason (transaction.go:821 "can't pool body for SendFrameDeferred — writeLoop holds reference") — so the hazard is acknowledged elsewhere but re-introduced here for the synchronous body buffer.

(3) writeLoop still reads body after ctx cancel: conn.go:517-521 outer select (and the drain at 534-546) can pick the buffered writeCh frame even when ctx.Done() is simultaneously ready — Go select is uniformly random when both are ready, and the drain's `case req=<-c.writeCh` is preferred over `default` whenever a frame is buffered. It then calls WriteFrame(c.wbuf, req.token, req.body) → framing.go:71 `copy(buf[payloadStart+16:], body)`, a READ of body's backing array.

(4) caller returns body to a global pool on that exact error path: commitpath.go:57 `marshalBufPool.Put(poolBuf)` (identical shape at readpath.go:484 getValueBufPool and readpath.go:856 getKeyValuesBufPool). A concurrent goroutine B does marshalBufPool.Get() → MarshalFDBPooled writes it IN PLACE (commitid_generated.go writes into `dst` when cap(dst)>=totalSize).

Happens-before: A's channel-send→writeLoop-receive orders writeLoop's read after A's marshal; A's Put→B's Get orders B's write after A's Put; A's send is sequenced-before A's Put on goroutine A. So both writeLoop's read AND B's write are "after A's send," but on divergent branches with NO edge between them → they are concurrent. Concurrent read (writeLoop WriteFrame copy) and write (B's MarshalFDBPooled) on one shared backing array = a genuine data race, UB under the Go memory model and detectable by `go test -race`.

Severity low: the only consumer of the garbled bytes is WriteFrame writing to the wbuf of the connection that was just torn down (frame discarded); goroutine B's own marshal output is untouched (a concurrent read can't corrupt B's write). So no data corruption / no wrong commit — the impact is a -race-detectable race that can surface as a CI flake during connection teardown (fault/chaos tests), not a wire/correctness divergence. Not a C++ divergence (Go-intrinsic), so cppReference n/a. No existing test pins it: the *_test.go files cover SendFrameDeferred-on-closed-conn (fault_test.go:899) and tenant commit pooling, but none exercise the synchronous-SendFrame ctx.Done-after-enqueue body-Put race under -race.

surviving.title: SYSTEM_IMMEDIATE + USE_GRV_CACHE: Go always forces a fresh GRV where libfdb_c serves the (up to 100ms stale) cached version
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/grv.go:284 (cache-block guard `if !isImmediate && useGrvCache && !skipGrvCache`, with isImmediate set at grv.go:277) and the second guard in tryCache at grv.go:74-75 (returns (0,false) for grvPrioritySystemImmediate)
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/NativeAPI.actor.cpp:7484-7486 (rkThrottlingCooledDown returns true for IMMEDIATE) → 7504-7506 (cache gate entered for IMMEDIATE+useGrvCache) → 7514-7517 (cached read version returned when fresh). Option-reachability gate: 7145-7153 (USE_GRV_CACHE legal at API<720 without sharedStatePtr).
surviving.description: In libfdb_c, an IMMEDIATE-priority transaction that set USE_GRV_CACHE still hits the cache: getReadVersion's gate (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:7504-7517) requires rkThrottlingCooledDown(priority), and rkThrottlingCooledDown returns true for TransactionPriority::IMMEDIATE (7485-7486), so the gate is entered and a fresh cached version (requestTime-lastTime<=100ms) is returned. Go deliberately bypasses the cache for SYSTEM_IMMEDIATE in two places: getReadVersion sets isImmediate (grv.go:277) and excludes it from the cache block (grv.go:284 `if !isImmediate && useGrvCache && !skipGrvCache`), and tryCache returns (0,false) for grvPrioritySystemImmediate (grv.go:74-75). So Go always issues a real GRV for IMMEDIATE.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ serves IMMEDIATE+useGrvCache from the cache (rkThrottlingCooledDown(IMMEDIATE)=true -> gate entered -> cached version returned); Go forces a fresh proxy GRV. Same warm cache + same options yield a different read version (cached V1 in C++ vs fresh V2 in Go) when the cluster advanced between cache-warm and the IMMEDIATE read. Manifests at API<720 (where USE_GRV_CACHE is otherwise allowed without shared state). The Go behavior is deliberate (documented) but diverges from the spec.
surviving.repro: At API version <720 (USE_GRV_CACHE allowed without shared state): (1) warm db.grvCache to version V1 via a DEFAULT-priority GRV or a commit; (2) within 100ms, have another client advance the cluster to V2 (any commit); (3) start a transaction, set PRIORITY_SYSTEM_IMMEDIATE and USE_GRV_CACHE, and read its read version. libfdb_c serves the cached V1 with no proxy contact (cache hit); Go issues a fresh proxy GRV and returns V2. Differential assertion: read-version equality (C++=V1 vs Go=V2) and/or cache-hit counter / proxy-contact observation (C++ hits cache, Go always contacts the proxy for IMMEDIATE).
surviving.verifierReasoning:
I read both sides in full.

C++ (/tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/NativeAPI.actor.cpp):
- 7484-7486: `rkThrottlingCooledDown(cx, priority)` returns `true` unconditionally when `priority == TransactionPriority::IMMEDIATE`.
- 7501-7506: `TransactionState::getReadVersion` opens with the cache gate as its FIRST statement — there is no IMMEDIATE early-out before it. The gate requires `!FORCE_GRV_CACHE_OFF && !options.skipGrvCache && (random01<=DEBUG_USE_GRV_CACHE_CHANCE || options.useGrvCache) && rkThrottlingCooledDown(cx, options.priority)`. For an IMMEDIATE txn that set USE_GRV_CACHE, all four are satisfied (the 4th via 7485-7486), so the gate IS entered.
- 7511-7517: it reads the shared cached version and returns it when `requestTime - lastTime <= MAX_VERSION_CACHE_LAG (100ms)` and `rv != 0`. So libfdb_c serves IMMEDIATE+useGrvCache from the (up-to-100ms-stale) cache.
- 7145-7153: USE_GRV_CACHE only throws invalid_option at apiVersionAtLeast(withGrvCache())==720 without a sharedStatePtr; at API<720 the option is legal, so the IMMEDIATE+USE_GRV_CACHE combination is reachable.

Go (pkg/fdbgo/client/grv.go):
- 277: `isImmediate := b.priority == grvPrioritySystemImmediate`.
- 284: `if !isImmediate && useGrvCache && !skipGrvCache {` — IMMEDIATE is excluded from the entire cache-serve block, so it always falls through to a real proxy GRV.
- 74-75: `tryCache` returns `(0,false)` for `grvPrioritySystemImmediate` as a second guard.

Concrete difference on input Z (API<720; cache warm at V1 with lastTime within 100ms; cluster advanced to V2; txn priority=IMMEDIATE + SetUseGrvCache; read its read version): C++ returns cached V1 (7514-7516, no proxy contact); Go forces a fresh GRV and returns V2. The Go behavior is deliberate and documented but contradicts the spec.

This is NOT refuted by existing tests: TestGRVCache_SystemImmediateBypass (grv_unit_test.go:48) and the "system immediate always bypasses cache" case (client_test.go:578-586) assert the Go-only behavior — they are unit tests pinning the divergent behavior, not a differential proving Go==libfdb_c, so they cement the divergence rather than catch it. No differential_*_test.go pins this shape.

Severity low: confined to API<720 (USE_GRV_CACHE needs sharedStatePtr at >=720) and the rare IMMEDIATE+USE_GRV_CACHE combination; both versions are valid read versions — Go merely pays an extra proxy round-trip and returns a fresher version, never returning wrong data, so it is a behavioral/performance divergence, not a wire-compat or correctness defect.

surviving.title: Atomic() with an invalid op-code AND an out-of-legal-range key returns invalid_mutation_type (2018) instead of key_outside_legal_range (2004) / client_invalid_operation (2000)
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1375 (op-validity poison set + early return, before mutation buffering at 1382); manifested at the commit gate transaction.go:1502-1503, which returns 2018 before the metadataVersionKey check (1564) and the legal-range loop (1582). Finder's location is correct.
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2226-2235 (atomicOp gate order: metadataVersionKey 2226-2229 / legal-range 2230-2231 BEFORE op-validity 2234). Supporting: getMaxWriteKey at ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2764-2769 (normalKeys.end == \xff); CATCH_AND_DIE abort wrapper at bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:1149 + macro 137-146. Finder's citation is correct.
surviving.description: Go's Atomic() runs the op-code validity gate (isAtomicOp -> sets invalidAtomicOpErr=2018 and DROPS the mutation, line 1375) before any legal-range/metadataVersionKey consideration, and Commit checks invalidAtomicOpErr (line 1502) before the per-mutation legal-range loop (line 1582). libfdb_c's RYW atomicOp checks the metadataVersionKey arm and `key >= getMaxWriteKey()` FIRST (lines 2226-2232) and only THEN the op-validity gate `!isValidMutationType || !isAtomicOp -> throw invalid_mutation_type` (line 2234). So when a single Atomic() call has BOTH an invalid op-code and a key that is out-of-legal-range (a 0xff system key with no system access) or is the metadataVersionKey, C++ throws 2004 (legal-range) or 2000 (metadataVersionKey) respectively, while Go returns 2018. The existing test TestAtomic_RejectsNonAtomicOpCode only covers invalid op-codes on NORMAL (in-range) keys, where 2018 is correct; the out-of-range-key sub-shape is unpinned.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ atomicOp precedence is metadataVersionKey/legal-range (2226-2232) > op-validity (2234); Go inverts it (op-validity at 1375 / commit 1502, legal-range loop at 1582). Provable in Go via asymmetry: Atomic(MutAddValue [valid], 0xff-key) commits as 2004 (legal-range loop), but Atomic(MutSetValue [invalid op-code], 0xff-key) commits as 2018 — C++ returns 2004 for both.
surviving.repro: Go-only regression (the live C binding aborts via CATCH_AND_DIE, so this pins Go to the RYW-spec error code rather than running a black-box differential). Open a transaction with NO system-key access (writeSystemKeys=false). Case A (legal-range): tx.Atomic(MutSetValue, []byte{0xff}, []byte("x")); err := tx.Commit(ctx) — Go currently yields FDBError code 2018 (invalid_mutation_type); RYW atomicOp spec yields 2004 (key_outside_legal_range), since key >= getMaxWriteKey()==\xff is checked at line 2231 before op-validity at 2234. Case B (metadataVersionKey): tx.Atomic(MutClearRange, []byte("\xff/metadataVersion"), []byte("x")); tx.Commit(ctx) — Go yields 2018; RYW spec yields 2000 (client_invalid_operation) from line 2228. Control (must stay 2018, both sides agree): an in-range key with a non-atomic op, e.g. tx.Atomic(MutSetValue, []byte("k"), []byte("x")) -> 2018. Fix direction: in Go's Atomic(), evaluate the metadataVersionKey/legal-range condition before the isAtomicOp gate (mirror RYW 2226-2234 ordering) so the both-invalid case surfaces 2004/2000.
surviving.verifierReasoning:
The precedence inversion is real and provable from both sources.

C++ RYW atomicOp (read /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2219-2235): the order of gates is (1) `if (key == metadataVersionKey)` -> `throw client_invalid_operation()` (2000) unless it's exactly SetVersionstampedValue+metadataVersionRequiredValue (lines 2226-2229); (2) `else if (key >= getMaxWriteKey())` -> `throw key_outside_legal_range()` (2004) (lines 2230-2231); and ONLY THEN (3) `if (!isValidMutationType(operationType) || !isAtomicOp(...))` -> `throw invalid_mutation_type()` (2018) (line 2234). getMaxWriteKey() for a non-system txn returns normalKeys.end == \xff (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2764-2769), so a key of []byte{0xff} satisfies `key >= getMaxWriteKey()`.

Go (read pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go): Atomic() runs the op-validity gate FIRST — line 1375 `if !isAtomicOp(op)` sets tx.invalidAtomicOpErr = {Code: 2018} and returns at 1379 BEFORE the mutation is ever buffered (the `tx.mutations = append(...)` is at 1382, after the return). Commit() then returns that poison at line 1502-1503, which is BEFORE the metadataVersionKey check (1564, code 2000) and the legal-range loop (1582, code 2004). isAtomicOp(MutSetValue=0) is false because bit 0 isn't in atomicOpMask (transaction.go:155-165); maxWriteKey() returns []byte{0xff} with no writeSystemKeys (transaction.go:1136-1141), matching C++.

Concrete divergence on a single Atomic() call carrying BOTH defects:
- Atomic(MutSetValue, []byte{0xff}, "x") then Commit: Go -> 2018 (invalid_mutation_type, from line 1502). C++ RYW atomicOp -> 2004 (key_outside_legal_range, line 2231), op-validity at 2234 unreached.
- Atomic(MutClearRange, []byte("\xff/metadataVersion"), "x") then Commit: Go -> 2018. C++ RYW atomicOp -> 2000 (client_invalid_operation, line 2228).

Caveat on how the spec is observed (does not refute the finding): fdb_transaction_atomic_op wraps the throw in CATCH_AND_DIE (bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:1149; macro at 137-146 prints "Unexpected FDB error %d" with e.code() and abort()s). So the running C binding ABORTS for both 2018 and 2004 — the differing value is the RYW-thrown e.code(), not a returned error. Go deliberately diverges from "abort" by deferring + rejecting the commit (documented at transaction.go:1367-1374), specifically so the bad mutation never reaches the shared cluster. Given that intentional design, the spec-faithful deferred code for the both-invalid input is the one RYW's atomicOp would throw (2004 / 2000), because RYW evaluates legal-range/metadataVersionKey before op-validity. Go surfaces 2018, so the deferred code is wrong for this sub-shape — a precedence bug nested inside an already-intentional design divergence.

Coverage check: TestAtomic_RejectsNonAtomicOpCode (pkg/fdbgo/client/atomic_invalid_opcode_test.go) only uses in-range user keys (prefix+"a", prefix+"set"), where 2018 is correct on BOTH sides, so it does not exercise the out-of-range / metadataVersionKey sub-shape. No Atomic() call combining an invalid op-code with an out-of-range or metadataVersionKey key exists in client/ or bench/differential_*_test.go (the only Atomic+0xff hits are valid ops with 0xff in the VALUE: apiversion_atomic_test.go:46 MutAnd, multishard_test.go:632 MutOr). The sub-shape is genuinely unpinned.

Severity low: this is a programmer-misuse path (non-atomic op-code passed to atomicOp). Both engines reject; no data corruption and no wire-format impact. The only observable difference is which non-retryable client error code is surfaced (2018 vs 2004/2000).

surviving.title: Single-key Clear of an oversized system key is silently dropped instead of rejected with key_outside_legal_range
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1294 (size-drop gate in Clear(), preceding any legal-range check; commit-loop legal-range gate it bypasses is at transaction.go:1582-1585)
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2419-2424 (legal-range throw at 2419-2420 before size-drop at 2422-2424); getMaxWriteKey() at ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2764-2769; CATCH_AND_DIE abort at bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:137-146 with fdb_transaction_clear at 1153-1155
surviving.description: Go's Clear() checks the SIZE first (len(key) > getMaxClearKeySize) and, if oversized, drops the clear and returns without buffering any mutation. libfdb_c's actual write path is ReadYourWritesTransaction::clear(KeyRef), which checks `key >= getMaxWriteKey()` -> throw key_outside_legal_range (line 2419) BEFORE the oversize-drop `key.size() > getMaxClearKeySize` (line 2422). The Go code's comment cites NativeAPI.actor.cpp:6045-6047 (the bare NativeAPI clear, which has no legal-range check) but libfdb_c never calls that directly — it goes through RYW, which checks legal-range first. So for a key that is BOTH oversized AND >= getMaxWriteKey (a >30000-byte key starting with 0xff, with no ACCESS_SYSTEM_KEYS), C++ raises key_outside_legal_range (2004; CATCH_AND_DIE in fdb_c.cpp:1154 aborts), whereas Go drops it silently and the commit succeeds as a no-op. For a NORMAL-sized (<=30000 byte) system-key Clear, Go correctly defers 2004 to commit (the mutation is buffered and the commit loop catches m.Key>=maxWrite); only the oversized case bypasses that legal-range gate via the early size-drop.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ RYW clear(KeyRef) order is legal-range (2419) then size-drop (2422); Go inverts it (size-drop at 1294, legal-range deferred to commit), so an oversized protected system-key clear escapes the legal-range check entirely. The divergence is provable purely in Go via internal asymmetry: a 30000-byte 0xff Clear commits as 2004, a 30001-byte 0xff Clear commits as nil.
surviving.repro: Unit test (no cluster needed, mirrors sizelimits_test.go harness): tx := newTestTx(); tx.tenantId = NoTenantID (no SetAccessSystemKeys → writeSystemKeys=false). Case B (the bug): tx.Clear(append([]byte{0xff}, bytes.Repeat([]byte("a"), 30000)...)) (len 30001, system key); tx.Commit(context.Background()) → Go returns nil. Case A (contrast, currently correct): tx.Clear(append([]byte{0xff}, bytes.Repeat([]byte("a"), 29999)...)) (len 30000); tx.Commit() → Go returns code 2004 (key_outside_legal_range). Expected per libfdb_c (RYW::clear legal-range at 2419 before size-drop at 2422; CATCH_AND_DIE abort with 2004): BOTH cases are key_outside_legal_range (2004). The len-30000-vs-30001 inconsistency for an identical 0xff prefix is the bug. Fix: in Clear(), do not apply the size-drop to a key that is >= tx.maxWriteKey(); buffer it so the commit-loop legal-range check surfaces 2004, matching C++ ordering.
surviving.verifierReasoning: Read Go Clear() (transaction.go:1290-1313): the first and only gate before buffering is the size-drop `if len(key) > getMaxClearKeySize(key)` at line 1294, which (sizelimits.go:43-45 → getMaxWriteKeySize(key,true)) returns 30000 for a 0xff-prefixed key. On oversize it consumes the no-conflict flag and returns WITHOUT buffering any mutation, so the legal-range check in the commit loop (transaction.go:1562 `maxWrite := tx.maxWriteKey()` = \xff without writeSystemKeys per maxWriteKey() at 1136-1141; 1582 `bytes.Compare(m.Key, maxWrite) >= 0` → code 2004) is never reached for the dropped key — Commit returns nil. Read C++ ReadYourWritesTransaction::clear (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2406-2438): the order is `if (key >= getMaxWriteKey()) throw key_outside_legal_range();` (2419-2420) BEFORE `if (key.size() > getMaxClearKeySize(key)) return;` (2422-2424). getMaxWriteKey() (2764-2769) returns normalKeys.end = \xff without writeSystemKeys, matching Go. A 0xff-prefixed key is >= \xff, so C++ throws key_outside_legal_range for BOTH a 30000-byte and a 30001-byte such key; fdb_transaction_clear (fdb_c.cpp:1153-1155) wraps it in CATCH_AND_DIE (137-146) → abort(). The divergence is provable inside Go via internal asymmetry: a 0xff+29999×'a' clear (len 30000, no ACCESS_SYSTEM_KEYS) is buffered → Commit=2004, while a 0xff+30000×'a' clear (len 30001) is dropped at line 1294 → Commit=nil. libfdb_c gives 2004 (abort) for both; Go silently no-ops the oversized one. Existing sizelimits_test.go drop tests (lines 199, 237) only use non-system 'a'-prefixed keys (key < \xff, where C++ also drops at 2422 — no divergence), so the 0xff oversized-clear axis is unpinned. The Go comment at 1291-1293 cites NativeAPI.actor.cpp:6045-6047 (the lower NativeAPI Transaction::clear), but libfdb_c reaches that only via RYW::clear after the legal-range gate, so the cited layer is the wrong one. Severity low: degenerate scenario (clearing a >30KB system key with no system access); neither path writes to the cluster, so no wire/data corruption — only error surfacing differs (C++ aborts loudly vs Go silently commits a no-op).
